mnemonics.title: 5NF Conditions
mnemonics.content: 5NF requires that Every Join Dependency Must be Implied By Candidate Keys - EJDMIC (Every Join Dependency Must Imply Candidate)

mnemonics.title: Normal Form Order
mnemonics.content: The order: 1NF → 2NF → 3NF → BCNF → 4NF → 5NF. Remember: People Seem To Build Concrete Foundations (People = Primary, Seem = Second, To = Third, Build = BCNF, Concrete = Fourth, Foundations = Fifth)

mnemonics.title: Lossless Join Test
mnemonics.content: For lossless join, common attributes must form a SuperKey of at least one relation - CSK (Common SuperKey)

mnemonics.title: 5NF vs 4NF
mnemonics.content: 4NF handles MVD (Multi-Valued), 5NF handles JD (Join Dependency) - MVD4, JD5 remember as 'May Visit Department 5'

acronyms.usage: Highest normal form addressing join dependencies, also called PJNF (Project-Join Normal Form)
Another name for 5NF, emphasizing the project and join operations used in decomposition
Constraint stating a relation equals the natural join of its projections on attribute sets
Dependency handled by 4NF before addressing JDs in 5NF
Stricter version of 3NF, handles certain anomalous FD cases
Basic dependency type handled by 1NF through BCNF

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a triangle where each corner represents a decomposed relation, and the center represents the original relation. The lines show how joining all three brings you back to the center without gaps (lossless) or with overlaps (lossy).

Draw three circles overlapping in a Venn diagram pattern - each circle overlaps with both others. This shows how A overlaps B, B overlaps C, and C overlaps A, forming a cycle that requires all three to reconstruct the original.

Use a matrix/table representation. Fill cells with either variables (for attributes in one relation only) or the number 1 (for common attributes). Apply chase test rules to see if you can derive a row of all 1s.
Stack building blocks: 1NF at base (atomic), 2NF-3NF above (keys and FDs), BCNF (superkeys), 4NF (MVDs), 5NF at top (JDs). Each level adds one more 'rule' to follow.

analogies.analogy: Think of a jigsaw puzzle - the original relation is the complete picture. Decomposing is breaking it into pieces. A lossless join is like putting the pieces back to form exactly the original image without missing pieces or extra pieces added.

Like organizing a library: you could keep all books in one big room, but it's easier to find books if split into Fiction, Non-Fiction, and Reference sections - but you need to remember which section each book came from (the join).
Think of three friends A, B, C where A knows B, B knows C, and C knows A - to reconstruct 'who knows whom' you need all three relationships; missing any creates an incomplete picture.
Like a prime number - it cannot be broken down further into smaller components that multiply to give the original. An irreducible JD cannot be split into smaller independent JDs.
